179	\section{Lifting a short exact sequence to injective resolutions}
180	
181	The passage from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
182	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) to a long exact sequence of
183	right-derived functors requires one geometric input: a short exact sequence of
184	objects must be lifted to a short exact sequence of their injective resolutions
185	which is, moreover, \emph{degreewise split}. This is the dual Horseshoe Lemma. It is the single genuinely new homological-algebra construction the argument
186	requires; individual injective resolutions (provided by enough injectives) furnish
187	the building blocks, and the horseshoe construction assembles them into the required
188	lift.
189	
190	\subsection{The horseshoe construction, step by step}
191	
192	The construction proceeds by induction on degree; we isolate its independent moving
193	parts as the sub-lemmas below, from which the horseshoe lemma is assembled. Throughout,
194	\(I_A\) and \(I_C\) are fixed injective resolutions of \(A\) and \(C\) (they exist
195	because \(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives), and we write
196	\(d_A, d_C\) for their differentials and \(I_A^n, I_C^n\) for their degree-\(n\) terms.
197	The middle resolution is built degreewise as the biproduct
198	\(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\). All seven statements are structural pieces of the dual
199	Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to Homological Algebra}, Lemma
200	2.2.8); no new source is introduced.
201	
202	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: a finite biproduct of injectives is injective]
203	  \label{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
204	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Injective.instBiprod}
205	  \mathlibok
206	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
207	  In an abelian category, if \(P\) and \(Q\) are injective objects then their biproduct
208	  \(P \oplus Q\) is injective. Consequently each degree term
209	  \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\) of the middle complex is injective, being the
210	  biproduct of the injective terms \(I_A^n\) and \(I_C^n\) of the two chosen
211	  resolutions.
212	\end{lemma}
213	
214	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: the biproduct short complex is split exact]
215	  \label{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}
216	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.Splitting.ofHasBinaryBiproduct}
217	  \mathlibok
218	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
219	  For objects \(P, Q\) of an abelian category the short complex
220	  \[
221	    P \xrightarrow{\ \iota_P\ } P \oplus Q \xrightarrow{\ \pi_Q\ } Q
222	  \]
223	  formed from the biproduct coprojection \(\iota_P\) and projection \(\pi_Q\) carries a
224	  canonical \texttt{ShortComplex.Splitting}; in particular it is a split short exact
225	  sequence. Applied in each degree \(n\) with \(P = I_A^n\), \(Q = I_C^n\), this exhibits
226	  every degree
227	  \[
228	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n \longrightarrow 0
229	  \]
230	  as split, furnishing the degreewise-splitting data required for the construction.
231	\end{lemma}

233	\begin{lemma}\leanok
234	[Per-stage monomorphism into the biproduct]
235	  \label{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
236	  \lean{CategoryTheory.mono_biprod_lift_factorThru_of_exact}
237	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
238	  Let \(0 \to S.X_1 \xrightarrow{S.f} S.X_2 \xrightarrow{S.g} S.X_3 \to 0\) be an exact
239	  short complex with \(S.f\) a monomorphism, and let \(\alpha : S.X_1 \to P\),
240	  \(\gamma : S.X_3 \to Q\) be monomorphisms with \(P\) injective. Then the map
241	  \[
242	    \bigl\langle\, \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)\,,\; S.g \circ \gamma \,\bigr\rangle
243	      \;:\; S.X_2 \longrightarrow P \oplus Q,
244	  \]
245	  whose first component is the injective extension of \(\alpha\) along the mono \(S.f\)
246	  and whose second component is \(S.X_2 \twoheadrightarrow S.X_3 \hookrightarrow Q\), is a
247	  monomorphism. This is the kernel/cokernel step driving each stage of the recursion:
248	  applied to the \(n\)-th cosyzygy short exact sequence with \(P = I_A^{n+1}\),
249	  \(Q = I_C^{n+1}\) it produces the monomorphism of the next cosyzygy into the next
250	  biproduct term, whose cokernel feeds the following stage (and, at the base stage with
251	  the original sequence \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\), the augmentation
252	  \(\beta : B \hookrightarrow I_B^0\)).
253	\end{lemma}
254	
255	\begin{proof}
256	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
257	  \leanok
258	  Write \(u := \langle \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f), S.g \circ \gamma\rangle\). To see
259	  \(u\) is mono it suffices, in an abelian category, to show \(x \circ u = 0 \implies x = 0\)
260	  for any \(x\). Composing \(x \circ u\) with the two biproduct projections gives
261	  \(x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\) and \(x \circ S.g \circ \gamma = 0\);
262	  since \(\gamma\) is mono the latter yields \(x \circ S.g = 0\). Exactness of \(S\) at
263	  \(S.X_2\) (with \(S.f\) mono) lets us lift \(x\) through \(S.f\): there is \(y\) with
264	  \(y \circ S.f = x\). Then \(y \circ \alpha = y \circ S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)
265	  = x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\), using the defining property
266	  \(S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = \alpha\); as \(\alpha\) is mono, \(y = 0\),
267	  hence \(x = y \circ S.f = 0\).
268	\end{proof}

281	  \[

283	  \]

299	  component of \(\beta\) extends the composite \(A \to I_A^0\) along the monomorphism

301	  \(B \twoheadrightarrow C \to I_C^0\).

303	  and seek \(\tau^{n+1} : I_C^{n+1} \to I_A^{n+2}\) with
304	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\). The right-hand side
305	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n : I_C^n \to I_A^{n+2}\) annihilates

309	  Indeed, precomposing with \(d_C^{n-1}\) and using the
310	  previous-stage identity \(\tau^{n} \circ d_C^{n-1} = -\,d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1}\) gives
311	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n \circ d_C^{n-1} = d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1} = 0\),
312	  where the final equality is the complex identity \(d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^{n} = 0\) for the

320	\end{proof}

489	\section{The dimension-shift step}
490	
491	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
492	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
493	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
494	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
495	complex-level homology long exact sequence
496	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
497	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We isolate the result as the kernel
498	sub-lemma below.
499	
500	\begin{lemma}[Dimension shift across an acyclic short exact sequence]
501	  \label{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
502	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic}
503	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
504	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
505	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
506	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
507	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
508	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
509	  % between abelian categories and assume
510	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
511	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
512	  % of $\mathcal{A}$.
513	  % \begin{enumerate}
514	  % \item If $A$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $B$.
515	  % \item If $A$ and $B$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $C$.
516	  % \item If $B$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ and $F(B) \to F(C)$ is
517	  % surjective then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$.
518	  % \end{enumerate}
519	  % In each of the three cases
520	  % $$
521	  % 0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to 0
522	  % $$
523	  % is a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{B}$."
524	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
525	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
526	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
527	  \[
528	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
529	  \]
530	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
531	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then:
532	  \begin{enumerate}
533	    \item the connecting maps of the long exact sequence of right-derived functors
534	      furnish isomorphisms
535	      \[
536	        (R^{k} G)(Z) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; (R^{k+1} G)(A)
537	        \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1;
538	      \]
539	    \item in the lowest degree there is an isomorphism
540	      \[
541	        (R^{1} G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr),
542	      \]
543	      i.e.\ \((R^1 G)(A)\) is the cokernel of the map \(G(J) \to G(Z)\) induced by
544	      the surjection \(J \twoheadrightarrow Z\).
545	  \end{enumerate}
546	\end{lemma}
547	
548	% The Stacks proof of Tag 015D derives the long exact sequence from the
549	% delta-functor structure of $RF$ on the derived category $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
550	% The proof below constructs the same sequence at the level of right-derived-functor
551	% objects from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
552	% (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe lift
553	% (Lemma \ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}); accordingly no verbatim proof
554	% quote from Tag 015D is attached.
555	\begin{proof}
556	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
557	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
558	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
559	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
560	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
561	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
562	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact
563	  sequence via a horseshoe lift.
564	
565	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
566	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
567	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
568	  complexes
569	  \[
570	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
571	  \]
572	  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
573	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
574	  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
575	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
576	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
577	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
578	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
579	  \[
580	    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
581	      \longrightarrow 0
582	  \]
583	  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
584	  complex-level homology long exact sequence
585	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
586	  \[
587	    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
588	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
589	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
590	  \]
591	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
592	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
593	  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
594	  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
595	  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
596	  sequence of right-derived functors
597	  \[
598	    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
599	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
600	  \]
601	  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
602	  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
603	  \[
604	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
605	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
606	  \]
607	
608	  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
609	  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
610	  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
611	  \[
612	    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
613	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
614	  \]
615	  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
616	  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism; this is part (1).
617	  In the lowest degree the segment
618	  \[
619	    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A)
620	      \to \underbrace{(R^1 G)(J)}_{=0}
621	  \]
622	  is exact with vanishing right end, so \(\delta^0\) is an epimorphism whose kernel is
623	  the image of \(G(J) \to G(Z)\); hence \(\delta^0\) identifies
624	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr)\) with \((R^1 G)(A)\), which is
625	  part (2).
626	\end{proof}

660	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015E (Leray's acyclicity
661	  lemma) and Tag 05TA (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
662	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives, \(\mathcal{B}\)
663	  abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) an additive left-exact functor.
664	  Let
665	  \[
666	    0 \longrightarrow A
667	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
668	      \longrightarrow \cdots
669	  \]
670	  be a resolution of \(A\) (an exact sequence) in which every object \(J^n\) is
671	  right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}. Then for
672	  every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\) at \(A\) is
673	  computed by the resolution: there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
674	  \[

676	  \]

678	  \(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to G(J^2) \to \cdots\) obtained by applying \(G\) degreewise
679	  to the augmentation-dropped resolution and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th cohomology
680	  object.

683	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
684	% $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
685	% $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
686	% for $n \geq n_0$. These sequences induce distinguished triangles
687	% $(J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1})$ in $D^+(\mathcal{A})$ by
688	% Lemma \ref{lemma-derived-canonical-delta-functor}.
689	% ... By Proposition \ref{proposition-derived-functor} we have a
690	% distinguished triangle $(RF(J^n), RF(I^{n + 1}), RF(J^{n + 1}))$.
691	% Since $J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1}$ are in $\mathcal{I}$
692	% the long exact cohomology sequence
693	% ... associated to this distinguished triangle collapses
694	% to an exact sequence
695	% $$

697	% $$

699	\begin{proof}
700	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
701	    lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
702	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
703	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
704	  The argument is the classical dimension shift, with the long exact sequence at each
705	  step supplied by the kernel Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}. Decompose the
706	  resolution into the
707	  short exact sequences obtained from its cosyzygies. Put \(Z^0 := A\) and, for
708	  \(m \geq 1\), let \(Z^m := \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr) =
709	  \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr)\) be the \(m\)-th cosyzygy; exactness
710	  of the resolution gives, for every \(m \geq 0\), a short exact sequence
711	  \[
712	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
713	      \longrightarrow 0, \tag{$S_m$}
714	  \]
715	  with \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\) and
716	  the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis.
717	
718	  \emph{Base degrees.} Applying the left-exact functor \(G\) to the resolution and
719	  using Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self} (\(R^0 G \cong G\)), the complex
720	  \(G(J^\bullet)\) has, in degrees \(0\) and \(1\),
721	  \[
722	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) = \ker\bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)\bigr) \cong G(A)
723	      = (R^0 G)(A),
724	  \]
725	  the last identification because \(G\) left exact carries
726	  \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to Z^1 \to 0\) to a left-exact sequence
727	  \(0 \to G(A) \to G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\) and \(Z^1 \hookrightarrow J^1\) makes
728	  \(\ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)) = G(A)\). For degree one,
729	  the dimension-shift Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(2) applied to the
730	  sequence \((S_0)\), \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to Z^1 \to 0\), gives
731	  \[
732	    (R^1 G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\bigr)
733	      \;\cong\; H^1\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
734	  \]

736	  image of \(G(Z^1)\) in \(G(J^1)\) is exactly \(\ker(G(J^1) \to G(J^2))\) (again by
737	  left-exactness of \(G\) applied to \((S_1)\)), so the cokernel of
738	  \(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\) coincides with \(\ker(G(J^1) \to G(J^2)) /
739	  \operatorname{im}(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^1(G(J^\bullet))\).
740	
741	  \emph{Induction (the shift).} For \(n \geq 1\), iterate the connecting
742	  isomorphism. The sequences \((S_0), (S_1), \ldots, (S_{n-1})\) all have acyclic
743	  middle terms, so Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(1) yields, for each
744	  \(1 \le m \le n-1\) and each \(k \geq 1\),
745	  \[

747	  \]

749	  \((R^{k} G)(Z^{1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) for \(k \geq 1\). Composing these
750	  isomorphisms down the staircase
751	  \[
752	    (R^n G)(A)
753	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
754	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
755	      \;\cong\; \cdots
756	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1}),
757	  \]
758	  reduces the computation of \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor, which
759	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(2) applied to \((S_{n-1})\),
760	  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), evaluates as
761	  \[
762	    (R^1 G)(Z^{n-1}) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr).
763	  \]

765	  \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\), identifies the inclusion
766	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) with \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\), whence
767	  \[
768	    \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr)
769	      \;\cong\;
770	      \frac{\ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr)}
771	           {\operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr)}
772	      \;=\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr).
773	  \]
774	  Combining the staircase with this last identification gives
775	  \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\) for all \(n \geq 1\); together with the
776	  base degrees this proves the theorem for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).
777	
778	  Every long exact sequence consumed above is the one produced by the kernel
779	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}, and is therefore grounded --- through the
780	  horseshoe lift (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) and the complex-level
781	  homology long exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) --- on
782	  the injective-resolution computation of \(R^n G\)
783	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). The injective objects supplied
784	  by ``enough injectives'' are themselves right-\(G\)-acyclic
785	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}), which is what makes an injective
786	  resolution a special case of an acyclic resolution and reconciles the present

@[simp]
noncomputable def mapComposableArrows₅ (i j : ι) (hij : c.Rel i j) :
    composableArrows₅ hS₁ i j hij ⟶ composableArrows₅ hS₂ i j hij :=
  homMk₅ (homologyMap φ.τ₁ i) (homologyMap φ.τ₂ i) (homologyMap φ.τ₃ i)
    (homologyMap φ.τ₁ j) (homologyMap φ.τ₂ j) (homologyMap φ.τ₃ j)
    (naturality' (mapComposableArrows₂ φ i) 0 1)
    (naturality' (mapComposableArrows₂ φ i) 1 2)
    (δ_naturality φ hS₁ hS₂ i j hij)
    (naturality' (mapComposableArrows₂ φ j) 0 1)
    (naturality' (mapComposableArrows₂ φ j) 1 2)

include hS₁ hS₂

lemma mono_homologyMap_τ₃ (i : ι)
    (h₁ : Epi (homologyMap φ.τ₁ i))
    (h₂ : Mono (homologyMap φ.τ₂ i))
    (h₃ : ∀ j, c.Rel i j → Mono (homologyMap φ.τ₁ j)) :
    Mono (homologyMap φ.τ₃ i) := by
  by_cases hi : ∃ j, c.Rel i j
  · obtain ⟨j, hij⟩ := hi
    apply mono_of_epi_of_mono_of_mono
      ((δlastFunctor ⋙ δlastFunctor).map (mapComposableArrows₅ φ hS₁ hS₂ i j hij))
    · exact (composableArrows₅_exact hS₁ i j hij).δlast.δlast
    · exact (composableArrows₅_exact hS₂ i j hij).δlast.δlast
    · exact h₁
    · exact h₂
    · exact h₃ _ hij
  · refine mono_of_epi_of_epi_of_mono (mapComposableArrows₂ φ i)
      (composableArrows₂_exact hS₁ i) (composableArrows₂_exact hS₂ i) ?_ h₁ h₂
    have := hS₁.epi_g
    apply epi_homologyMap_of_epi_of_not_rel
    simpa using hi

lemma epi_homologyMap_τ₃ (i : ι)
    (h₁ : Epi (homologyMap φ.τ₂ i))
    (h₂ : ∀ j, c.Rel i j → Epi (homologyMap φ.τ₁ j))
    (h₃ : ∀ j, c.Rel i j → Mono (homologyMap φ.τ₂ j)) :
    Epi (homologyMap φ.τ₃ i) := by
  by_cases hi : ∃ j, c.Rel i j
  · obtain ⟨j, hij⟩ := hi
    apply epi_of_epi_of_epi_of_mono
      ((δ₀Functor ⋙ δlastFunctor).map (mapComposableArrows₅ φ hS₁ hS₂ i j hij))
    · exact (composableArrows₅_exact hS₁ i j hij).δ₀.δlast
    · exact (composableArrows₅_exact hS₂ i j hij).δ₀.δlast
    · exact h₁
    · exact h₂ j hij
    · exact h₃ j hij
  · have := hS₂.epi_g
    have eq := (homologyFunctor C _ i).congr_map φ.comm₂₃
    dsimp at eq
    simp only [homologyMap_comp] at eq
    have := epi_homologyMap_of_epi_of_not_rel S₂.g i (by simpa using hi)
    exact epi_of_epi_fac eq.symm

lemma isIso_homologyMap_τ₃ (i : ι)
    (h₁ : Epi (homologyMap φ.τ₁ i))
    (h₂ : IsIso (homologyMap φ.τ₂ i))
    (h₃ : ∀ j, c.Rel i j → IsIso (homologyMap φ.τ₁ j))
    (h₄ : ∀ j, c.Rel i j → Mono (homologyMap φ.τ₂ j)) :
    IsIso (homologyMap φ.τ₃ i) := by
  have := mono_homologyMap_τ₃ φ hS₁ hS₂ i h₁ (IsIso.mono_of_iso _) (fun j hij => by
    have := h₃ j hij
    infer_instance)
  have := epi_homologyMap_τ₃ φ hS₁ hS₂ i inferInstance (fun j hij => by
    have := h₃ j hij
    infer_instance) h₄
  apply isIso_of_mono_of_epi

lemma quasiIso_τ₃ (h₁ : QuasiIso φ.τ₁) (h₂ : QuasiIso φ.τ₂) :
    QuasiIso φ.τ₃ := by
  rw [quasiIso_iff]
  intro i
  rw [quasiIsoAt_iff_isIso_homologyMap]
  apply isIso_homologyMap_τ₃ φ hS₁ hS₂
  all_goals infer_instance

module

public import Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.HomologySequence
public import Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.QuasiIso
public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.DiagramLemmas.Four

/-!
# Consequences of the homology sequence

Given a morphism `φ : S₁ ⟶ S₂` between two short exact sequences
of homological complexes in an abelian category, we show the naturality
of the homology sequence of `S₁` and `S₂` with respect to `φ`
(see `HomologicalComplex.HomologySequence.δ_naturality`).

Then, we shall show in this file that if two out of the three maps `φ.τ₁`,
`φ.τ₂`, `φ.τ₃` are quasi-isomorphisms, then the third is. We also obtain
more specific separate lemmas which give sufficient conditions for one
of these three morphisms to induce a mono/epi/iso in a given degree
in terms of properties of the other two in the same or neighboring degrees.

So far, we state only four lemmas for `φ.τ₃`. Eight more similar lemmas
for `φ.τ₁` and `φ.τ₂` shall also be obtained (TODO).

-/

@[expose] public section

open CategoryTheory ComposableArrows Abelian

namespace HomologicalComplex

variable {C ι : Type*} [Category* C] [Abelian C] {c : ComplexShape ι}
  {S S₁ S₂ : ShortComplex (HomologicalComplex C c)} (φ : S₁ ⟶ S₂)
  (hS₁ : S₁.ShortExact) (hS₂ : S₂.ShortExact)
